section.title: How Is Your Beloved Better?
section.sectionRationale: The daughters of Jerusalem, hearing the bride sick with love, ask her a searching question: how is your beloved better than any other? Her answer is the one full portrait of the man in the whole Song, a praise from head to foot that ends, this is my beloved, and this is my friend. When the friends offer to seek him with her, she already knows where he is and answers with the great refrain of belonging: I belong to my beloved and he belongs to me.
section.thesis: Asked to give a reason for her love, the bride answers with confident, wholehearted praise of her beloved as outstanding among ten thousand and altogether lovely, and rests in the settled certainty that she is his and he is hers.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the friends' question, how is your beloved better; the bride's head to foot praise of the one who is altogether lovely, her beloved and her friend; and the answer to where he has gone, sealed by the refrain, I belong to my beloved and he belongs to me.

verses.text: How is your beloved better than others, O {{yafah|most beautiful}} among women? How is your beloved better than another, that you charge us so?

units.label: The praise of the beloved
units.note: outstanding among ten thousand, altogether lovely

verses.text: My {{dodi|beloved}} is dazzling and ruddy, outstanding among ten thousand.
His head is purest gold; his hair is wavy and black as a raven.
His eyes are like doves beside the streams of water, bathed in milk and mounted like jewels.
His cheeks are like beds of spice, towers of perfume. His lips are like lilies, dripping with flowing myrrh.
His arms are rods of gold set with beryl. His body is polished ivory bedecked with sapphires.
His legs are pillars of marble set on bases of pure gold. His appearance is like Lebanon, as majestic as the cedars.
His mouth is most sweet; he is altogether lovely. This is my beloved, and this is my {{rea|friend}}, O daughters of Jerusalem.

units.label: Where he has gone, and the refrain of belonging
units.note: I belong to my beloved and he belongs to me

verses.text: Where has your beloved gone, O most beautiful among women? Which way has he turned? We will seek him with you.
My beloved has gone down to his garden, to the beds of spices, to pasture his flock in the gardens and to gather lilies.
I belong to my beloved and he belongs to me; he pastures his flock among the lilies.

yafah.gloss: Beautiful, fair. The daughters address the bride as the most beautiful among women even as they press her question; her beauty is not in doubt, only whether her beloved is worth such devotion.

dodi.gloss: My beloved. The bride's recurring name for the man, literally my love or my dear one. It runs through the Song as the language of settled, mutual belonging, my beloved is mine and I am his.

rea.gloss: Friend, companion. At the end of her praise the bride says, this is my beloved, and this is my friend, joining passion and friendship: the one she desires is also the one she most delights to be with.

basic.context: The daughters of Jerusalem have heard the bride say she is sick with love, and now they ask her a fair question: what makes your beloved so special, better than anyone else? Her answer is the only full description of the man in the whole Song. She praises him from head to foot, dazzling, majestic, altogether lovely, and sums it up, this is my beloved, and this is my friend. When they offer to help her look for him, she already knows: he has gone down to his garden. She rests in the great refrain, I belong to my beloved and he belongs to me.
basic.hermeneutics: This is love speaking well of the one it loves. On the plain level it is a wife's glad testimony to her husband's worth when someone asks her to give a reason. The imagery is poetic praise, not a literal photograph. Within the whole Bible, the church has long heard in this passage the believer's confident answer when the world asks, what is so special about your Lord, and has found here language for the surpassing beauty of Christ, outstanding among ten thousand, altogether lovely.
basic.originalLanguages: The friends call the bride yafah, most beautiful, yet the question is about her dodi, her beloved. She answers not with argument but with adoring description, ending on the word rea, friend, so that the one she desires is also the one she most delights to be with. The refrain, I belong to my beloved and he belongs to me, is the settled heart of the Song.
basic.typology: The bride asked to say why her beloved is better pictures the church called to give a reason for her hope. Her wholehearted praise of the one altogether lovely foreshadows the believer's testimony to Christ, the chief among ten thousand, in whom is found every beauty.
basic.christ: The bride's beloved is outstanding among ten thousand and altogether lovely, language the church has long lifted to Christ. When the world asks, how is your beloved better, the believer answers as she does, by pointing to Him: dazzling, majestic, the fairest of all, and adds, this is my beloved, and this is my friend. He is both the Lord we adore and the friend who calls us friends.
basic.god: God gives His people not only commands but a Beloved to delight in, and He is not ashamed to be spoken of with such glad affection. He welcomes the testimony of a heart that finds Him better than all others, and He is Himself the friend of sinners who draws near.
basic.crossReferences: 1 Peter 3:15, always be ready to give a reason for the hope within you. Psalm 45:2, the king fairer than the sons of men. John 15:15, no longer servants but friends. Philippians 3:8, counting all things loss for the surpassing worth of knowing Christ.
basic.oneStory: When the world asks the church what makes her Lord worth such devotion, she answers as the bride does, with confident praise of the one who is altogether lovely, her beloved and her friend, and rests in the certainty that she is His and He is hers.

context.literary: A dialogue that turns into a wasf, a descriptive praise poem, this time the bride praising the man, the only sustained portrait of the bridegroom in the Song. It is framed by the friends' question at 5:9 and their offer at 6:1, and it closes with the refrain of mutual belonging at 6:3, which echoes 2:16.
context.historical: The praise draws on the wealth and craft of the ancient world: purest gold, beryl and sapphires, polished ivory, pillars of marble, and the cedars of Lebanon. To liken the beloved to such things is to say he is precious, strong, and kingly, the finest imaginable.
context.canonical: The confident testimony to a surpassing beloved runs through Scripture, from the king fairer than the sons of men in Psalm 45, to the one altogether lovely here, to the Lamb who alone is worthy in Revelation. The refrain I am my beloved's and my beloved is mine anticipates the covenant bond of Christ and His church.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show love giving a glad reason for itself: when challenged to say why her beloved surpasses all others, the bride answers with wholehearted praise and rests in settled, mutual belonging.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage describes the bride's praise rather than commanding it, yet it models something abiding: love that can speak well and confidently of the one it loves, and that finds its security in belonging to and being loved by the beloved.

debates.question: What kind of book is the Song: a celebration of married love, an allegory of Christ and the church, or both?

views.label: A celebration of covenant married love
views.case: Here a wife answers a plain question with glad praise of her husband and rests in belonging to him; Scripture openly honors such delight and faithfulness within marriage, Proverbs 5:18 to 19 and Genesis 2:24.

views.label: An allegory of Christ and the church, or God and Israel
views.case: The church has long heard in outstanding among ten thousand and altogether lovely the surpassing beauty of Christ, and in the refrain I am my beloved's the covenant bond, in keeping with Psalm 45 and Ephesians 5.

views.label: Both, the literal love as a God-given sign
views.case: The wisest reading holds both: the poem truly celebrates a wife's confident love for her husband, and because marriage is a God-given sign of Christ and the church, Ephesians 5:31 to 32, her praise of the one altogether lovely rightly lifts our eyes to Christ.

debates.bearing: Holding both keeps the passage honest: it honors a wife's glad testimony to her husband and follows it up to the Beloved who is truly outstanding among ten thousand and altogether lovely.

study.originalLanguages: The daughters call the bride yafah, most beautiful, but the question presses on her dodi, her beloved, the name of settled belonging that runs through the Song. She answers with adoring description, gold and jewels and cedar, and lands on the word rea, friend: this is my beloved, and this is my friend, joining desire and companionship in one breath. Then, asked where he is, she needs no search, for she rests in the refrain, I belong to my beloved and he belongs to me, the covenant heartbeat of the whole poem.

typology.type: The bride asked to say why her beloved is better
typology.fulfillment: The church called to give a reason for the hope within her, always ready to testify to her Lord, 1 Peter 3:15.

typology.type: The beloved outstanding among ten thousand, altogether lovely
typology.fulfillment: Christ, fairer than the sons of men, in whom all beauty is found, Psalm 45:2 and Colossians 1:19.

typology.type: The refrain, I belong to my beloved and he belongs to me
typology.fulfillment: The mutual belonging of Christ and His church, who are His and to whom He gives Himself, John 10:14 and Ephesians 5:29 to 30.

study.christ: The bride's answer becomes the church's confession. When the world asks how her beloved is better than any other, she points to the one who is dazzling and ruddy, outstanding among ten thousand, altogether lovely. The church has long borrowed these very words for Christ, the chief among ten thousand, fairer than the sons of men, in whom the fullness of God was pleased to dwell. And she names Him not only Lord but friend, the one who calls His people friends and lays down His life for them. Her rest, I belong to my beloved, is the believer's rest in the covenant love of Christ.
study.god: God does not merely require obedience; He gives His people a Beloved to delight in and welcomes their glad testimony to His worth. He is not embarrassed to be praised with such affection. In Christ He shows Himself both the Lord who is altogether lovely and the friend of sinners who draws near, so that His people can say with confidence, this is my beloved, and this is my friend.

crossReferences.note: Always be prepared to give a reason for the hope that is in you, with gentleness and respect, as the bride answers the question put to her.
The king is fairer than the sons of men, grace poured out on his lips, language echoed in the praise of the altogether lovely beloved.
No longer servants but friends, echoing this is my beloved, and this is my friend.
My beloved is mine and I am his, the refrain of belonging repeated here at 6:3.

study.oneStory: Asked to say why her beloved surpasses all others, the bride does not argue but adores, praising the one who is altogether lovely and naming him her beloved and her friend. Her confident testimony and her rest in mutual belonging point beyond every earthly love to Christ, outstanding among ten thousand, in whom the church finds every beauty and to whom she gladly belongs.

quiz.q: What question do the daughters of Jerusalem put to the bride?

options.text: How is your beloved better than any other, that you charge us so to find him?
options.note: Their question sets up the bride's whole praise of the beloved.

options.text: Why have you stopped loving your beloved?
options.note: Her intense state does prompt their question, but she has not stopped loving him; she is sick with love and longs for him.

options.text: Where did you first meet your beloved?
options.note: They are curious about her beloved, as this question is, but they ask what makes him better than any other, not where she first met him.

options.text: Why is your beloved angry with you?
options.note: There is no anger here; the question is about his surpassing worth.

quiz.q: How does the bride describe her beloved in 5:10?

options.text: Dazzling and ruddy, outstanding among ten thousand.
options.note: She praises him as surpassing all others, language the church lifts to Christ.

options.text: Ordinary, but pleasant enough.
options.note: He is indeed pleasant, but far from ordinary; she calls him dazzling and outstanding among ten thousand.

options.text: One among many equals.
options.note: She does set him among ten thousand others, but she lifts him out as outstanding among them, not merely one among many equals.

options.text: Fading and frail.
options.note: She praises his strength and majesty, like marble and cedar.

quiz.q: How does the bride sum up her beloved at the end of her praise, in 5:16?

options.text: This is my beloved, and this is my friend.
options.note: She joins desire and friendship, the one she loves and delights to be with.

options.text: This is my master, and I am only his servant.
options.note: She is indeed wholly devoted to him, but she names him her beloved and her friend, not a master she serves beneath.

options.text: This is a stranger I hardly know.
options.note: She knows him deeply and calls him her friend.

options.text: This is one of many I admire.
options.note: She does admire him greatly, but he is her one beloved, altogether lovely, not one among many she admires.

quiz.q: What refrain of belonging does the bride return to in 6:3?

options.text: I belong to my beloved and he belongs to me.
options.note: The covenant heartbeat of the Song, mutual belonging, repeated from 2:16.

options.text: My beloved belongs to everyone alike.
options.note: Their love is exclusive and mutual, not shared with all.

options.text: I hope one day to belong to my beloved.
options.note: She does confess belonging to her beloved, but as a settled present reality, not merely a hope for one day.

options.text: My beloved once belonged to me.
options.note: The belonging is real, but it is present and secure, not a thing of the past that once was.

quiz.q: How does this passage point beyond marriage to the gospel?

options.text: The church answers the world's question by praising Christ, altogether lovely, her beloved and friend.
options.note: The bride's confident testimony pictures the believer giving a reason for her hope in Christ.

options.text: It teaches that we should never speak to others about who we love.
options.note: The passage is indeed about how she speaks of her beloved, but it commends glad testimony to his worth, not silence about whom we love.

options.text: It shows that God finds no beauty worth praising.
options.note: The passage overflows with praise of the beloved's surpassing beauty.

options.text: It has no connection to Christ or the church.
options.note: The passage truly praises a human beloved, yet the church has long heard the surpassing worth of Christ in outstanding among ten thousand and altogether lovely, so it does point beyond marriage.

questions: The daughters ask, how is your beloved better than any other? If someone asked you what makes Christ worth your devotion, how would you answer?
The bride praises her beloved from head to foot without embarrassment. When did you last speak gladly and specifically about the worth of the one you love, whether a spouse or the Lord?
She calls him my beloved and my friend in one breath. How does it steady you to know that Christ is both the Lord you adore and the friend who calls you His own?
I belong to my beloved and he belongs to me. How would resting in that mutual belonging change the way you face a hard day this week?
The bride knew where her beloved had gone, to his garden. Where do you go to meet with the Lord, and how might you seek Him there more deliberately?

translationNotes.note: The friends' question, how is your beloved better than another, invites the praise that follows; it is a real question, not mockery.
The Hebrew rendered mouth is literally palate; the phrase this is my friend joins covenant love with deep companionship.
I belong to my beloved and he belongs to me repeats the refrain of 2:16, the settled language of mutual, exclusive belonging.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Song of Solomon 5:9-6:3, spanning chapters 5 and 6. Verse text assembled by concatenating segment text values in order; embedded speaker labels such as The Friends and The Bride were treated as headings and excluded. No em or en dashes occur in this section; none remain.
